title: Primitive types and variables

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java primitive variables hold values from fixed language-defined types, local variables require definite assignment, fields receive defaults, and final prevents reassignment after initialization.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A measurement uses double, an exact count uses int after a range check, and a local final conversion factor documents that the binding will not be reassigned. Your goal is to choose and use primitive types while explaining range, precision and default-value differences. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: First source and main; pf state expressions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java primitive variables hold values from fixed language-defined types, local variables require definite assignment, fields receive defaults, and final prevents reassignment after initialization. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Integer ranges are fixed but arithmetic can overflow, floating values approximate many decimals, char is a UTF-16 code unit, and var infers only a local static type from an initializer. The learner must reject this documented misconception: All numeric primitives expand automatically to preserve mathematical results, and local primitives silently start at zero.

includes: Declare initialized primitive values, constants and inferred local variables with appropriate types.
Choose and use primitive types while explaining range, precision and default-value differences.
excludes: Integer ranges are fixed but arithmetic can overflow, floating values approximate many decimals, char is a UTF-16 code unit, and var infers only a local static type from an initializer.

outcomes: Choose and use primitive types while explaining range, precision and default-value differences.

examples.title: Primitive types and variables: direct language case
examples.situation: A measurement uses double, an exact count uses int after a range check, and a local final conversion factor documents that the binding will not be reassigned.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A measurement uses double, an exact count uses int after a range check, and a local final conversion factor documents that the binding will not be reassigned.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Choose a primitive by its specified range and precision, initialize every local before use, and check overflow or precision when the domain requires it.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Choose a primitive by its specified range and precision, initialize every local before use, and check overflow or precision when the domain requires it.

examples.title: Primitive types and variables: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A supplementary Unicode symbol occupies two char values, so code-point-aware APIs are used instead of assuming one char always means one visible character.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A supplementary Unicode symbol occupies two char values, so code-point-aware APIs are used instead of assuming one char always means one visible character.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Integer ranges are fixed but arithmetic can overflow, floating values approximate many decimals, char is a UTF-16 code unit, and var infers only a local static type from an initializer.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: All numeric primitives expand automatically to preserve mathematical results, and local primitives silently start at zero.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and use primitive types while explaining range, precision and default-value differences.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: All numeric primitives expand automatically to preserve mathematical results, and local primitives silently start at zero.
misconceptions.correction: Choose a primitive by its specified range and precision, initialize every local before use, and check overflow or precision when the domain requires it.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: All numeric primitives expand automatically to preserve mathematical results, and local primitives silently start at zero.

usefulFor: tracing primitive values, operators, decisions, and loop progress
limitations: the panel does not model promotion, overflow, short-circuit evaluation, scope, or JVM representation exactly

recap.summary: You should now be able to choose and use primitive types while explaining range, precision and default-value differences. Keep this boundary in view: Integer ranges are fixed but arithmetic can overflow, floating values approximate many decimals, char is a UTF-16 code unit, and var infers only a local static type from an initializer.
